elements.sent-2411-001: The angular measure on the unit sphere characterizes the first-order dependence structure of the components of a random vector in extreme regions and is defined in terms of standardized margins.

elements.section-2411-002: Regular variation and the angular measure
elements.sent-2411-054: Let $F_1$ and $F_2$ denote the marginal cumulative distribution functions of $X_1$ and $X_2$, respectively, assumed to be continuous.
elements.sent-2411-055: As in \cite{einmahl2001,einmahl2009maximum , our working hypothesis is that the distribution of the standardized random vector \begin{equation}U = (U_1,U_2) = (1-F_1(X_1), 1-F_2(X_2)),\end{equation} is multivariate regularly varying in the sense that there exists a Radon measure $\Lambda$ on $\spacerv \egdef [0,\infty]^2 \setminus \{(\infty,\infty)\}$ such that \begin{equation}\label{eq:Lambda} t^{-1} \PP \lc U/t \in \cdot \, \rc \xrightarrow{v} \Lambda(\,\cdot\,), \qquad t \ra 0,\end{equation} where $\xrightarrow{v}$ denotes vague convergence of measures, see~\cite[Section~3.4]{resnick1987 .}}
elements.sent-2411-056: This is equivalent to assume that $U$ is in the minimal domain of attraction of a min-stable distribution with standardized margins~\cite[Proposition~5.17]{resnick1987 .}
elements.sent-2411-057: In terms of sets, vague convergence is equivalent to the following property: for any Borel set $B \subset \spacerv$ bounded away from the point $(\infty,\infty)$ and with $\Lambda(\partial B) = 0$, we have \begin{equation}\label{eq:regular_variation_sets} \lim_{t \ra 0} t^{-1} \PP \lc U \in tB \rc = \Lambda(B),\end{equation} where $tB = \{(tx,ty): (x,y) \in B\}$.
elements.sent-2411-058: The limiting measure obtained through this standardization is often denoted by $\nu$ and is referred to as the exponent measure.
elements.sent-2411-060: Below, we list some properties of the measure $\Lambda$ which will be useful later:.
elements.sent-2411-061: The angular measure is a finite measure derived from the exponent measure and with support contained in the intersection of the unit sphere and the positive orthant $[0,\infty)^2$.
elements.sent-2411-062: Even though the angular measure can be defined with respect to any norm, we will only consider $L_p$-norms, the ones most used in practice, which are defined, for $p \in [1,\infty]$ and $x = (x_1,x_2) \in \R^2$, by \begin{equation}\label{eq:p-norms} \|x\|_p \egdef \begin{dcases} (|x_1|^p+|x_2|^p)^{1/p} \text{if $1 \leq p < \infty$,} \max(x_1,x_2) \text{if $p = \infty$.} \end{dcases}\end{equation} For $p \in [1,\infty]$, we will let $\Phi_p$ denote the angular measure associated to the measure $\Lambda$ in \eqref{eq:Lambda , defined on any Borel set $A \subset [0,\pi/2]$ by \begin{equation}\label{eq:ang_meas_def} \Phi_p(A) \egdef \Lambda \lp \{(x_1,x_2) \in (0,\infty]^2: \|(x_1^{-1},x_2^{-1})\|_p \geq 1, \arctan(x_2/x_1) \in A \} \rp.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-063: The inverses appearing in the radial component come from the fact that $\Phi_p$ is usually defined in terms of $\nu$ in \eqref{eq:nu rather than $\Lambda$.}
elements.sent-2411-064: If, for $\theta \in [0,\pi/2]$, we introduce the sets \begin{equation}\label{eq:Cptheta} C_{p,\theta} \egdef \begin{dcases} ([0,\infty] \times \{0\}) \cup (\{\infty\} \times [0,1]) \text{if } \theta = 0, \lacc (x,y) : 0 \leq x \leq \infty, 0 \leq y \leq \min\{x \tan\theta, y_p(x)\} \racc \text{if } 0 < \theta < \pi/2, \lacc (x,y) : 0 \leq x \leq \infty, 0 \leq y \leq y_p(x) \racc \text{if } \theta = \pi/2, \end{dcases}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}y_p(x) \egdef \begin{dcases} \infty if  x \in [0,1), \lp 1 + \frac{1}{x^p-1} \rp^{1/p} if  x \in [1,\infty]  and  p \in [1,\infty), 1 if  x \in [1,\infty]  and  p = \infty, \end{dcases}\end{equation} then it follows that \begin{equation}\Phi_p(\theta) \egdef \Phi_p([0,\theta]) = \Lambda(C_{p,\theta}).\end{equation}.

elements.section-2411-005: Testing the Hüsler--Reiss model for river discharge data
elements.sent-2411-217: We consider the danube dataset from the R package graphicalExtremes \cite{graphicalExtremes .}
elements.sent-2411-218: Average daily discharges are recorded at 31 gauging stations in the upper Danube basin covering parts of Germany, Austria and Switzerland which are often affected by flooding.
elements.sent-2411-219: To not have to deal with seasonality, we only consider the daily discharges in the summer months (June, July and August), and we use the declustered time series from the data\_clustered dataset.
elements.sent-2411-220: The same data have already been analyzed by several authors, e.g.~\cite{hu2024 , who estimate bivariate dependence structures using the Hüsler--Reiss model for connected pairs of stations after estimating a Markov tree on the full dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-221: The topographic map of the upper Danube basin which can be found in Figure~1 of~\cite{asadi2015 is displayed in Figure~\ref{fig:danube together with the associated flow chart of its river network.}}
elements.sent-2411-222: Following the approach of~\cite{hu2024 , our goal is to assess the goodness-of-fit of the Hüsler--Reiss model to all $m = 30$ adjacent pairs in the flow chart of Figure~\ref{fig:danube_fc .}}
elements.sent-2411-223: Naturally, this leads to multiple testing challenges due to the many dependent tests involved.
elements.sent-2411-224: The p-values can be adjusted using either the classical Bonferroni correction or the False Discovery Rate (FDR) paradigm of Benjamini and Hochberg~\cite{benjamini1995 , adapted for dependent tests in~\cite{benjamini2001 .}}
elements.sent-2411-225: The sample size is $n = 428$, and we set $k = \sqrt{n}$ as we observed in Section~\ref{sec:simu-HR that this typically leads to better results for the nonparametric estimation of $Q_p$, while still providing a reasonable estimate of the nuisance parameter $r$, with respect to which the quantile values $\quantile_{r (1-\alpha)$ do not vary too quickly.}}
elements.sent-2411-226: Ideally, we would like to use two different values of $k$ for the nonparametric and parametric estimators in the test statistic $T_n$, as a larger $k$ often improves the accuracy of the parameter estimators.
elements.sent-2411-227: This, however, is a topic for future research.
elements.sent-2411-228: We estimate the p-values $P_j$ corresponding to each of the $j \in \{1,\ldots,m\}$ tests by \begin{equation}\widehat{P}_j = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \1 \lacc T_n > L_{\widehat{r}_n}^{(b)} \racc\end{equation} for $B=4\,000$, where $L_{\widehat{r _n ^{(b)}$ is drawn from the distribution of $L_r$ in Theorem~\ref{thm:asymptoticTS at $r=\widehat{r _n$, computed in the same way as in Section~\ref{sec:simu-HR .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-229: Although alternative parameter estimators could be used, this would result in more complex expressions for $I_{g,\sigma,r}$ in the definition of $T_n$, and we aimed to keep the methodology simple.

elements.sent-2411-236: We propose a new inference method based on the angular measure to evaluate whether the asymptotic dependence structure of a bivariate dataset aligns with a given parametric model.
elements.sent-2411-237: Unlike previous approaches, our method involves sampling directly from the asymptotic distribution of the test statistic under the estimated parameter value.
elements.sent-2411-238: Leveraging continuity of the asymptotic quantile function, this leads to a detailed proof of the method's consistency.
elements.sent-2411-239: The finite-sample performance is also compelling when compared to other techniques, and the ease of computing the test statistic makes it a strong candidate for practical applications.
elements.sent-2411-240: Although the method is currently limited to bivariate data, many multivariate extreme value methods rely on multiple bivariate analyses, and we demonstrate how this can be done on a real dataset.

elements.section-2411-012: Sampling from the asymptotic distribution of the test statistic
elements.sent-2411-421: In this section, we briefly describe how to generate samples from the asymptotic distribution of $T_n$ in Theorem~\ref{thm:asymptoticTS , as it could be of independent interest.}
elements.sent-2411-422: The underlying principle is not new and has been used, e.g., in~\cite{einmahl2001 to draw confidence bounds for the empirical angular measure based on its asymptotic distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-423: However, no detailed procedure could be found in the literature and our aim is to fill in this gap.
elements.sent-2411-424: As in the proof of Lemma~\ref{lem:cont_limit_law in Appendix~\ref{sec:app:cont_quantiles , the key observation is that the random variable $L_{r_0}$ appearing as the limit in distribution of $T_n$ can be written as \begin{equation}L_{r_0} = \int_0^{\pi/2} \labs X_{r_0}(\theta) \rabs \, q(\theta) \, \d\theta,\end{equation} for some process $X_{r_0 \in \ell^\infty([0,\pi/2])$ which is a linear transformation of the Gaussian process $W_{\Lambda_{r_0 }$ introduced before Theorem~\ref{thm:asymptoticsEAM .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-425: From now on, we will omit the subscript $r_0$ to alleviate the notation.
elements.sent-2411-426: The random variable $L$ will be approximated by the Riemann sum \begin{equation}L \approx \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^N |X(\theta_i)| \, q(\theta_i)\end{equation} for a large equidistant grid $0 < \theta_1 < \ldots < \theta_N < \pi/2$.
elements.sent-2411-428: Hence, to obtain a large number $B$ of simulated values for $L$, it suffices to generate $B$ trajectories of the process $X$ on the chosen grid.
elements.sent-2411-429: In Section~\ref{sec:simulations , we choose $B=2\, 000$ while the $p$-value computations in Section~\ref{sec:application are based on $B=4\, 000$ replications.}}
elements.sent-2411-430: We give an overview of the procedure below.
elements.sent-2411-431: The first step is to obtain a realization of the underlying process $W_\Lambda$ by considering a sufficiently large rectangular grid of points $\{(x_i,y_j) : i,j = 1,\ldots,M\}$ on $\spacerv$ of the form \begin{equation}x_i = (i-1) h \qquad \text{and} \qquad y_j = (j-1) h,\end{equation} for a small step size $h > 0$.
elements.sent-2411-433: These points are used to define the cells \begin{equation}C_{ij} \egdef \lacc (x,y) \in \spacerv : x \in [x_i, x_{i+1}) \text{ and } y \in [y_j, y_{j+1}) \racc, \qquad i,j \in \{1, \ldots, M-1\},\end{equation} which are considered as an approximate disjoint covering of the space $\spacerv$.
elements.formula-2411-209: \begin{equation}C_{ij} \egdef \lacc (x,y) \in \spacerv : x \in [x_i, x_{i+1})  and  y \in [y_j, y_{j+1}) \racc, \qquad i,j \in \{1, \ldots, M-1\},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-434: The fact that the cells do not cover points $(x,y) \in \spacerv$ for which $x \vee y > (M-1) h$ is not supposed to affect the results too much as the variance of $W_\Lambda$ is negligible on this part of the space.
elements.sent-2411-435: Associated with those cells, we generate a matrix $W = (W_{ij )_{i,j = 1, \ldots, M-1}$ of independent centered Gaussian random variables with $\Var(W_{ij ) = \Lambda(C_{ij )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-436: The property $\EE[W_\Lambda(C) W_\Lambda(C')] = 0$ for disjoint Borel sets $C,C' \subset \spacerv$ justifies the approximation \begin{equation}\label{eq:process_disc} \tW_\Lambda(C) \egdef \sum_{i=1}^{M-1} \sum_{j=1}^{M-1} W_{ij} \, \1\{(x_i,y_j) \in C\}\end{equation} for any Borel set $C \subset \spacerv$ to obtain a realization of the process $W_\Lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-437: Of particular interest are sets of the form $C = C_{p,\theta}$ for some $1 \leq p \leq \infty$ and $\theta \in [0,\pi/2]$, for which formula~\eqref{eq:process_disc simplifies to \begin{equation}\tW_\Lambda(C_{p,\theta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{M-1} \sum_{j=1}^{M-1} W_{ij} \, \1 \{y_j \leq \min(y_p(x_i), x_i\tan\theta)\} = \sum_{i=1}^{M-1} \sum_{j=1}^{ \lc \tfrac{y_p(x_i)}{h} + 1 \rc \wedge (M-1)} W_{ij} \, \1 \lacc (j-1) \leq (i-1) \tan \theta \racc,\end{equation} where $[x]$ denotes the integer part of $x \in \R$.}
elements.sent-2411-438: Similar approximations based on~\eqref{eq:process_disc can be found for the marginal processes $W_1$ and $W_2$, leading to \begin{equation}\tW_1(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{\lc \frac{x}{h} + 1 \rc \wedge (M-1)} W_{i \bullet} \quad and \quad \tW_2(y) = \sum_{j=1}^{\lc \frac{y}{h} + 1 \rc \wedge (M-1)} W_{\bullet j},\end{equation} where $W_{i \bullet \egdef \sum_{j=1 ^{M-1 W_{ij}$ and $W_{\bullet j \egdef \sum_{i=1 ^{M-1 W_{ij}$ for $(i,j) \in \{1,\ldots, M-1\}^2$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-439: For $C = A_{(x,y)}$ as introduced in Assumption~\ref{ass:stdf_expansion and appearing in the formula for $X(\theta)$, we get \begin{equation}\tW_\Lambda \lp A_{(x,y)} \rp = \tW_1(x) + \tW_2(y) - \sum_{i=1}^{\lc \frac{x}{h} + 1 \rc \wedge (M-1)} \sum_{j=1}^{\lc \frac{y}{h} + 1 \rc \wedge (M-1)} W_{ij}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-440: As the random variable $X(\theta)$ consists of a linear transformation of the process $W_\Lambda$ evaluated on sets of the above forms, these formulas will lead to a convenient approximation for the random vector $\lp X(\theta_1), \ldots, X(\theta_N) \rp$ as scalar products between $W$ and weights, possibly depending on $\theta$, which can be computed independently of the generation of $W$, before the evaluation of the scalar products, which can be done in an efficient way using parallel computing.
elements.sent-2411-441: More details can be found on the GitHub repository of the first author \footnote{E-mail \href{mailto:stephane.lhaut@uclouvain.be {stephane.lhaut@uclouvain.be if the repository is not yet available at the time of reading.. .}}}
